title: Results at a glace: Evaluation of the Autism Spectrum Disorder Program 2018-19 to 2021-22 (Public Health Agency of Canada) - Canada.ca
content:
## Results at a glace: Evaluation of the Autism Spectrum Disorder Program 2018-19 to 2021-22 (Public Health Agency of Canada)
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that can include impairments in speech, non-verbal communication, and social interactions, combined with restricted and repetitive behaviours, interests, or activities. Each person with ASD is unique, and the term "spectrum" refers to the wide variety of strengths and challenges reflected among those with the disorder. In 2019, 2% of Canadian children and youth aged 1 to 17 years were diagnosed with autism, with males four times more likely to be diagnosed with autism than females.
The Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) is one of many organizations addressing ASD in Canada. Other key players include local public health authorities, provincial and territorial (P/T) governments, other government departments, and national and community-based organizations.
PHAC's ASD program is led by the Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch. Funding and policy activities are led by the Centre for Health Promotion, including the ASD Strategic Fund. Surveillance activities fall under the Centre for Surveillance and Applied Research. PHAC's planned spending on ASD activities for the evaluation period was approximately $4.5 million per year.

## Evaluation approach and methods
The purpose of the evaluation was to examine the relevance and effectiveness of PHAC's ASD activities with a focus on achievement of results, including progress towards planned outcomes.
- Document and file review
- Key informant interviews
- Comparative analysis with other jurisdictions
- Financial data review

## What the evaluation found
To date, PHAC has undertaken activities to address ASD that are well aligned with its federal public health role. Although PHAC used a social determinants of health approach to determine funding priorities for the Strategic Fund, some projects would have been better suited under a different jurisdiction. Important gaps remain and, although most of them fall under P/Ts' mandate, some gaps appear to align with the federal public health role in the areas of convening, documenting promising practices, and enhancing surveillance.
Although community-based projects were short in duration, they made some notable advancements by increasing the knowledge and skills of program participants and, in some cases, helping autistic individuals develop coping strategies, and helping practitioners and caregivers improve support. Projects were promising, but the short-term nature of funding did not allow them to reach their full potential or ensure the sustainability and scalability of operations and activities. The Autism and/or Intellectual Disability Knowledge Exchange Network built an online portal, developed and shared multiple online resources, and established several networking hubs across Canada; however, there is limited evidence of uptake of these activities. Finally, the PHAC-funded guidelines on pediatric assessment of ASD were released in 2019, filling an important gap in progressing toward a more consistent approach to diagnosing ASD across Canada.
PHAC has made important advancements in terms of ASD national surveillance, including establishing reporting on indicators beyond prevalence, and expanding geographical coverage. The ongoing actions taken to address current data gaps (e.g., collecting data on all ages and in all P/Ts) might increase surveillance data uptake and thus lead to informed decision making. Within PHAC, there are opportunities to strengthen internal decision making once ASD data become more complete.
- Ensure that gaps related to greater needs that fall within the federal public health role are addressed by PHAC in the upcoming National Autism Strategy.
- Enhance ASD Initiative design and performance measurement.
- Continue to strengthen national ASD surveillance efforts with a focus on establishing internal ASD priorities and plans.
